mnemonics.title: Array Index Starts at 0
mnemonics.content: ZERO IS THE HERO - Array indices in C ALWAYS start at 0, not 1. Remember: first element is at index 0, second at index 1, and so on.

mnemonics.title: Array Size Formula
mnemonics.content: SIZE MINUS ONE - Maximum valid index = array size minus 1. For arr[10], valid indices are 0 to 9.

mnemonics.title: Binary Search Prerequisites
mnemonics.content: MUST BE SORTED - Binary search ONLY works on sorted arrays. Unsorted = use linear search.

mnemonics.title: Linear vs Binary Search
mnemonics.content: LINEAR CHECKS ALL, BINARY SPLITS IN HALF - Linear: O(n) for unsorted. Binary: O(log n) for sorted.

acronyms.usage: Remember that the last accessible index in an array of size N is N-1, not N.

acronyms.expansion: Sequential Check Each And Report - Linear search checks elements sequentially until found or exhausted.

visualTips.visualization: Think of an array as a hotel with consecutive room numbers starting from 0. Room 0 is the first room, Room 1 is the second, etc. Each room (element) has the same size (same data type).
Draw an array as boxes in a row, side by side with no gaps. Each box has a room number (index) written below it and the value stored inside.
Imagine looking for a word in a dictionary. You open in the middle, decide which half contains your word, then repeat. Each step eliminates half the remaining pages.

analogies.analogy: Like a parking lot with numbered spaces. The array name is the parking lot, each space is an element, and the index is the space number. You find your car by knowing its space number.
Like searching for a book on an unsorted bookshelf by checking each book from left to right until you find the right one.
Like searching for a word in a dictionary by opening to the middle, deciding whether your word comes before or after, and repeating with the appropriate half.
